title: - BYZANTINE-ROBUST LEARNING ON HETEROGE NEOUS DATASETS VIA RESAMPLING [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - BYZANTINE-ROBUST LEARNING ON HETEROGE NEOUS DATASETS VIA RESAMPLING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In Byzantine robust distributed optimization, a central server wants to train a machine learning model over data distributed across multiple workers. However, a fraction of these workers may deviate from the prescribed algorithm and send arbitrary messages to the server. While this problem has received significant attention recently, most current defenses assume that the workers have identical data. For realistic cases when the data across workers are heterogeneous (non-iid), we design new attacks which circumvent these defenses leading to significant loss of performance. We then propose a simple resampling scheme that adapts existing robust algorithms to heterogeneous datasets at a negligible computational cost. We theoretically and experimentally validate our approach, showing that combining resampling with existing robust algorithms is effective against challenging attacks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Distributed or federated machine learning, where the data is distributed across multiple workers, has become an increasingly important learning paradigm both due to growing sizes of datasets, as well as privacy and security concerns. In such a setting, the workers collaborate to train a single model without transmitting their data directly over the networks (McMahan et al., 2016; Bonawitz et al., 2019; Kairouz et al., 2019). Due to the presence of either actively malicious agents in the network, or simply due to system and network failures, some workers may disobey the protocols and send arbitrary messages; such workers are also known as _Byzantine_ workers (Lamport et al., 2019). Byzantine robust optimization algorithms combine the gradients received by all workers using robust aggregation rules, to ensure that the training is not impacted by the malicious workers.

While this problem has received significant recent attention, (Alistarh et al., 2018; Blanchard et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2018a), most of the current approaches assume that the data present on each different worker has identical distribution. In this work, we show that existing Byzantine-robust methods catastrophically fail in the realistic setting when the data is distributed heterogeneously across the workers. We then propose a simple resampling scheme which can be readily combined with existing aggregation rules to allow robust training on heterogeneous data.

**Contribution.** Concretely, our contributions in this work are

- We show that when the data across workers is heterogeneous, existing robust rules might not converge, even without any Byzantine adversaries.

- We propose two new attacks, normalized gradient and mimic, which take advantage of data heterogeneity and circumvent median and sign-based defenses (Blanchard et al., 2017; Pillutla et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019).

- We propose a simple new resampling step which can be used before any existing robust aggregation rule. We instantiate our scheme with KRUM and theoretically prove that the resampling generalizes it to the setting of heterogeneous data.

- Our experiments evaluate the proposed resampling scheme against known and new attacks and show that it drastically improves the performance of 3 existing schemes on realistic heterogeneously distributed datasets.

**Byzantine attack model.** In each iteration, there is a set **Byz** of at most _f_ Byzantine workers. The remaining workers are **good** , thus follow the described protocol. A Byzantine worker _j ∈_ **Byz** can deviate from protocol and send an arbitrary vecter to the server. Besides, we also allow that Byzantine workers can collude with each other and know every state of the system. Unlike martingale-based approaches like (Alistarh et al., 2018), we allow the set **Byz** to change over time (Blanchard et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Mhamdi et al., 2018).

## 2 RELATED WORK

There has been significant recent work of the case when the workers have identical data distributions (Blanchard et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Mhamdi et al., 2018; Alistarh et al., 2018; Mhamdi et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2018a;b; Su & Xu, 2018; Damaskinos et al., 2019). We discuss the most pertinent of these methods next. Blanchard et al. (2017) formalize the Byzantine robust setup and propose a distance-based approach KRUM which selects a worker whose gradient is very close to at least half the other workers. A different approach involves using the median and its variants (Blanchard et al., 2017; Pillutla et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2018a). Yin et al. (2018a) propose to use and analyze the coordinate-wise median method (CM). Pillutla et al. (2019) use a smoothed version of Weiszfeld’s algorithm to iteratively compute an approximate geometric median of the input gradients. In a third approach, (Bernstein et al., 2018) propose to use the signs of the gradients and then aggregate them by majority vote, however, (Karimireddy et al., 2019) show that it may not always converge. Finally, Alistarh et al. (2018) use a martingale-based aggregation rule which gives a sample complexity optimal algorithm for iid data. The distance-based approach of KRUM was later extended in Mhamdi et al. (2018) who propose BULYAN to overcome the _dimensional leeway_ attack. This is the so called _strong Byzantine resilience_ and is orthogonal to the question of non-iid-ness we study here. Recently, (Peng & Ling, 2020; Yang & Bajwa, 2019a;b) studied Byzantine-resilient algorithms in the decentralized setting where there is no central server available. Extending our techniques to the decentralized setting is an important direction for future work.

In a different line of work, (Lai et al., 2016; Diakonikolas et al., 2019) develop sophisticated spectral techniques to robust estimate the mean of a high dimensional multi-variate standard Gaussian distribution where samples are evenly distributed in all directions and the attackers are concentrated in one direction. Very recent work (Data & Diggavi, 2020) extend the theoretical analysis to non-convex, strongly-convex and non-i.i.d setup under a gradient dissimilarity assumption and propose a gradient compression scheme on top of it. Our resampling trick can be combined with it to further reduce gradient dissimilarity.

Many attacks have been devised for distributed training. For the iid setting, the state-of-the-art attacks are (Baruch et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2019b). The latter attack is very strong when the fraction of adversaries is large (nearly half), but in this work we focus on settings when this fraction is quite small (e.g. _≤_ 0 _._ 2). Further our normalized mean attack Section 3.2 is inspired by (Xie et al., 2019b). The former work focuses on attacks which are coordinated across time steps. Developing strong practical defenses even in the iid case against such time-coordinated attacks remains an open problem. In this work, we sidestep this issue by restricting ourselves to new attacks made possible by non-iid data and studying how to overcome them. We focus on schemes which work in the iid setting, but fail with non-iid data. Once a new method which can defend against (Baruch et al., 2019) is developed, our proposed scheme shows how to adapt such a method to the important non-iid case. For the non-iid setting, backdoor attacks are designed to take advantage of heavy-tailed data and manipulate

model inference on specific subtask, rather than lower the overall accuracies of training (Bagdasaryan et al., 2018; Bhagoji et al., 2018). In contrast, this paper is not intended to address aforementioned challenges but rather to defend the attacks that lower the training accuracies in the non-iid setting.

As far as we are aware, only (Li et al., 2019; Ghosh et al., 2019; Sattler et al., 2020) explicitly investigate Byzantine robustness with non-iid workers. Li et al. (2019) proposes an SGD variant (RSA) which modifies the original objective by adding an _ℓ_ 1 penalty. Ghosh et al. (2019); Sattler et al. (2020) assume that all workers belong to an apriori fixed number of clusters and use an outlier-robust clustering method to recover these clusters. If we assume that the server has the entire training dataset and can control the distribution of samples to good workers, Xie et al. (2019a); Chen et al. (2018); Rajput et al. (2019) show that non-iid-ness can be overcome. Typical examples of this is distributed training of neural networks on public cloud, or volunteer computing Meeds et al. (2015); Miura & Harada (2015). However, none of these methods are applicable in the standard federated learning setup we consider here. We aim to minimize the original loss function over workers while respecting the non-iid data locality, i.e. the partition of the given heterogeneous dataset over the workers, without data transfer.

## 3 ATTACKS AGAINST EXISTING AGGREGATION SCHEMES

In this section we show that when the data across the workers is heterogeneous (non-iid), then we can design new attacks which take advantage of the heterogeneity, leading to the failure of existing aggregation schemes. We study three classes of robust aggregation schemes: i) schemes which select a representative worker in each round (e.g. KRUM (Blanchard et al., 2017)), ii) schemes which use normalized means (e.g. RSA (Li et al., 2019)), and iii) those which use the median (e.g. RFA (Pillutla et al., 2019)). We show realistic settings under which each of these classes would fail when faced with heterogeneous data.

- 3.1 FAILURE OF REPRESENTATIVE WORKER SCHEMES ON NON-IID DATA

Algorithms like KRUM select workers who are representative of a majority of the workers, by relying on statistics such as pairwise differences between the various worker updates. Let ( _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n_ ) be the gradients by the workers, _f_ of which are Byzantine (e.g. _n ≥_ 2 _f_ + 3 for KRUM). For _i ̸_ = _j_ , let _i → j_ denote that _**g** j_ belongs to the _n − f −_ 2 closest vectors to _**g** i_ . Then KRUM is defined as follows

However, when the data across the workers is heterogeneous, there is no ‘representative’ worker. This is because each worker computes their local gradient over vastly different local data. Hence, for convergence it is important to not only select a good (non-Byzantine) worker, but also ensure that each of the good workers is selected with roughly equal frequency. Hence KRUM suffers a significant loss in performance with heterogeneous data, even when there are _no Byzantine workers_ .

For example, when KRUM is used for iid datasets without adversary ( _f_ =0, see left of Figure 1a), the test accuracy is close to simple average and the gap can be filled by MULTI-KRUM (Blanchard et al., 2017). The right plot of Figure 1a also shows that KRUM’s selection of gradients is biased towards certain nodes. When KRUM is applied to non-iid datasets (the middle of Figure 1a), KRUM performs poorly even without any attack. This is because KRUM mostly selects gradients from a few nodes whose distribution is closer to others (the right of Figure 1a). This is an example of how robust aggregation rules may fail on realistic non-iid datasets.

- 3.2 ATTACKS ON NORMALIZED AGGREGATION SCHEMES

Instead of simply averaging the gradients, some methods first normalize them and then average. This limits the influence of the Byzantine workers since they cannot output extremely large gradients, and hence is more robust. On the right side of Figure 1b, we can see that this attack lowers the accuracy of RFA-T1 significantly, as the number of Byzantine workers increases. Comparing to its iid counterpart, the normalized mean attack is even more impactful in the non-iid setting.

3.3 ATTACKS ON MEDIAN-BASED SCHEMES

Geometric median and its variants are popular in robust learning research (Blanchard et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Pillutla et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2018a; Mhamdi et al., 2018). Given gradients _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ , we use the estimator

If the vectors _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ are drawn independently from the same distribution, intuitively most of them would concentrate around their mean. Then, even if there are some Byzantine outputs, the median would ignore those as outliers and output a ‘central’ point close to the mean.

However, when _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ are gradients over heterogeneous data, they may be vastly different from each other and do not concentrate around the mean. In such a scenario, the median such as (7) can be even less robust than simply taking the mean. Suppose that worker 0 is Byzantine and the remaining workers _{_ 1 _, . . . ,_ 2 _n}_ are good, with a total of 2 _n_ + 1 workers. Now suppose that _gi_ = ( _−_ 1) _[i]_ for all the workers, with half the good workers having _−_ 1 and the other half +1. This means that the true mean is 0, however, the median estimator (7) will output 1.

**Mimic attack.** This motivates our _mimic_ attack in which all Byzantine workers collude and agree to always send gradients from the same worker. We define a specialized attack, called _mimic2_ , where half of the good workers have same datasets and send _**g**_ 1 while the rest good workers send _**g**_ 2; then all Byzantine workers send _**v**_ = _**g**_ 1 such that the geometric median of the gradients received by the server is always _**g**_ 1. Therefore, this attack breaks geometric-median-based robust aggregation rules, by leading them to wrong solutions. The left plot of Figure 1c shows the impact of the mimic2 attack. Test accuracies of CM and RFA both drop drastically to around 50%.

## 4 ROBUST AGGREGATION ON NON-IID DATA

In Section 3 we have demonstrated how existing robust aggregation rules can fail in realistic non-iid scenarios, with and without attackers (Sections 3.2 and 3.3 and Section 3.1 respectively). To overcome this problem, we propose a simple new resampling-based aggregation rule for training, shown in Algorithm 1. More specifically, we choose _s-resampling without replacement_ in Algorithm 2 where each gradient can be sampled at most _s_ times. The key property of our rule is that after resampling, the resulting set of averaged gradients _{_ _**g**_ ¯ _t_ : _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] _}_ are much more homogeneous (lower variance). Then these averaged gradients are fed to existing Byzantine robust aggregation schemes, such as KRUM, see Section 5. Given an existing aggregation rule AGGR, we denote by AGGR _◦_ Resampling the resulting new robust aggregation rule for non-iid input gradients.

**Remark 1.** _For s_ = 1 _, resampling simply becomes shuffling of the input elements, and var_ (¯ _**g** t_ ) = _σ_[2] _is unchanged. For s >_ 1 _, the resampling scheme reduces the heterogeneity (variance) by approximately_ 1 _/s. Thus, increasing s leads to the resulting resampled gradients being a better estimator of the population mean, thus improving training convergence speed. On the other hand, increasing s also increases the number of resampled gradients which can be affected by a Byzantine worker. In particular, if f workers are Byzantine, then up to fs resampled gradients can be incorrect, which has to be taken into account by the employed robust aggregation rule. In practice, we found that using a small value s_ = 2 _was already sufficient to overcome heterogeneity._
**Remark 2.** _A natural question to ask is what happens if we resample with replacement but do not limit on the number of replacements. We discuss this additional algorithm variant in Appendix C._

Note that the _{_ _**g**_ ¯ _t_ : _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] _}_ are identically distributed but not independent. This does not directly fit into the original assumptions of Byzantine robust algorithms like KRUM and hence the robustness has to be reproved for our more general setting.

## 5 CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS WITH KRUM

In this section, we analyze the convergence of SGD with robust aggregation on non-iid data. Since the definition of robustness and other conditions vary from paper to paper, it is not possible to give a uniform proof perfectly fit for all methods. For example, (Yin et al., 2018a) assumes the gradients have bounded variance and skewness whereas others like KRUM, RFA, BULYAN does not. Thus we only analyze KRUM for its simplicity and popularity, and show that analysis is only slightly different from the original version. For other algorithms, we show by experiments that resampling helps them achieve better performance on heterogeneous data, see Section 6.

## 6 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we demonstrate the effect of resampling on datasets distributed in a non-iid fashion. Throughout the section, we illustrate the challenge, attacks, and defense by an example of training an MLP on the MNIST dataset (LeCun et al., 1998). In Appendix D, we present the results of similar experiments on Fashion-MNIST (Xiao et al., 2017) and CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009). The dataset is sorted by labels and sequentially divided into equal parts among good workers; Byzantine workers have access to the dataset on all good workers. Implementations are based on PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) and will be made publicly available.

## 6.1 RESAMPLING AGAINST THE ATTACKS ON NON-IID DATA

In Section 3 we have presented how heterogeneous data can lead to failure of existing robust aggregation rules. Here we apply our proposed resampling with _T_ = _n_ , _s_ =2 to the same aggregation rules, showing that resampling overcomes the described failures. Results are presented in Figure 2. In Figure 2a, we show that using resampling helps KRUM to achieve better test accuracy on noniid data. Since resampling KRUM with _s_ =2 actually averages 2 gradients, we compare it with MULTIKRUM with _m_ =2. The middle of Figure 2a shows that MULTIKRUM with _m_ =2 performs better than KRUM, but KRUM with resampling is even better which suggests the resampling step improves the performance on non-iid data. The selection histogram on the rightmost part of Figure 2a shows that after resampling, KRUM’s selection is much more evenly distributed between the good workers. In Figure 2b, we show that resampling fixes RFA with _T_ =1 and allows it to defend against the normalized mean attack. The resampling-based aggregation can almost reach same accuracy for both iid and non-iid setup. In Figure 2c, while mimic attack does not work for median-based rules in the iid setting, resampling still slightly improves the performance due to variance reduction. In the non-iid setting, resampling drastically improves the accuracy to the same level as the iid setting.

## 6.2 RESAMPLING AGAINST GENERAL BYZANTINE ATTACKS

In Figure 3, we present thorough experiments on non-iid data over 10 workers with 2 Byzantine workers. In each subfigure, we compare an aggregation rule with its variant with resampling. Three aggregation rules are compared: KRUM, CM, RFA. In particular, we compare to RFA with both T=1 (normalized mean) and T=8 (geometric median).

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical and timely challenge in Byzantine-robust distributed learning—namely, the vulnerability of existing robust aggregation methods under data heterogeneity, a common characteristic of real-world federated systems. The authors make a strong case for re-evaluating current defenses beyond the i.i.d. assumption by demonstrating both theoretically and empirically that classical robust aggregators such as KRUM, median, and sign-based methods fail severely in non-iid settings, even without active Byzantine attacks. Their construction of two novel attacks—normalized gradient and mimic—that exploit data heterogeneity to evade detection is particularly compelling and reveals important blind spots in current robustness paradigms. The proposed resampling mechanism is elegant in its simplicity, easily pluggable into existing defenses, and supported by theoretical justification showing improved convergence behavior under heterogeneity. The paper is clearly written, well-structured, and builds a convincing narrative across motivation, attack analysis, and defense. However, a key limitation lies in the somewhat restricted theoretical scope: the convergence analysis focuses on adapting KRUM under resampling but lacks broader formal guarantees for other base aggregators or more general non-convex settings, which limits the extent to which the theoretical results fully support the empirical breadth. Additionally, while the resampling step is computationally lightweight, the paper does not thoroughly discuss potential statistical inefficiencies or increased variance due to repeated sampling, especially in low-data regimes. Nonetheless, the work makes a meaningful contribution by bridging a crucial gap between theory and practice in robust federated learning, offering both a cautionary analysis and a practical remedy for heterogeneous environments.

comments.title: Flawed resampling algorithm with multiple technical and logical issues
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 is fundamentally underspecified and contains multiple issues: (1) The resampling loop uses a premature exit condition (when any gradient has been selected s times), potentially yielding batches with fewer than s gradients while still dividing by s, introducing bias. (2) The algorithm assumes T = n averages can be produced, but with a hard cap of s uses per gradient, fewer than T averages may be possible, especially for small s. (3) The notation is inconsistent: the computed average is **g**_t but returned as **g**_¯_t, creating confusion. (4) The pseudocode suggests resampling is i.i.d., but the shared counter across batches introduces dependence, invalidating independence assumptions. (5) The sampling mechanism does not resemble standard 'resampling without replacement' and is not guaranteed to produce T valid outputs. This undermines the reproducibility of the method and casts doubt on the theoretical claims depending on it.

comments.title: Incorrect and unjustified variance reduction claim in Proposition I
comments.quote: var(\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t) = \frac{n-1}{sn-1} \sigma^2 \quad \forall t \in [T]
comments.explanation: Proposition I claims the variance of the resampled gradient average is $ \frac{n-1}{sn-1} \sigma^2 $, but this expression is both incorrect and unjustified. The correct variance under finite-population sampling without replacement is $ \frac{\sigma^2}{s} \cdot \frac{n-s}{n-1} $. Moreover, the derivation assumes independence across resampled batches, but Algorithm 2 uses a global counter limiting each gradient to s uses, creating dependence between batches. This invalidates the use of i.i.d. averaging formulas. The paper references 'sampling theory' vaguely without addressing this dependence. Furthermore, Remark 1 claims variance reduces by 1/s, which only holds asymptotically and ignores the finite-population factor. These errors weaken the theoretical support for the claimed variance reduction and affect downstream results like Theorem II.

comments.title: Mismatch between theoretical estimator and algorithmic implementation in Definition A
comments.quote: Let G = G(\boldsymbol{\xi}) be an independent random variable which randomly selects a good worker i and samples a vector from Di and E G = \mathbf{g}.
comments.explanation: Definition A defines $ G $ as sampling a gradient from a single random good worker, so $ \mathbb{E}[G] = \frac{1}{n_g} \sum_{i \in \text{good}} \mu_i $, which only equals the true population mean $ \mu = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \nabla L_i(x) $ under uniform weights. In unbalanced or non-iid settings, this is not guaranteed. More critically, the aggregation input in Algorithm 1 consists of resampled *averages* of s gradients, not single worker gradients, so the population target for resilience should be $ \mu $, not $ \mathbb{E}[G] $. By not defining $ \mathbf{g} $ and conflating the estimator $ G $ with the actual resampled inputs, the paper undermines the applicability of the ($\alpha$, f)-Byzantine resilience framework. This gap between definition and implementation weakens the theoretical justification of the overall method.

While this appears to be a minor OCR or notation error, it introduces ambiguity, especially since the paper cites methods like RSA that use modified objectives. Without clarification, a reader might infer $ F_i $ represents a regularized or transformed version of $ L_i $. comments.title: Overstated and ambiguous mimic attack on geometric median
comments.quote: Then all Byzantine workers send $ \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{g}_1 $ such that the geometric median of the gradients received by the server is always $ \mathbf{g}_1 $.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the geometric median will output exactly $ \mathbf{g}_1 $ under the Mimic2 attack, where Byzantine workers send $ \mathbf{g}_1 $ to exploit a bimodal good gradient distribution. However, the geometric median is a continuous estimator and generally lies in the convex hull of inputs, not necessarily at one of them. In high dimensions, it equals $ \mathbf{g}_1 $ only under degenerate conditions (e.g., collinear gradients or extreme imbalance). The assertion that it is 'always $ \mathbf{g}_1 $' overstates the determinism of the attack and ignores the continuity of the estimator. While the attack may bias the median toward $ \mathbf{g}_1 $, claiming exact recovery misrepresents the behavior and risks assuming the conclusion. This weakens the theoretical strength of the attack model.

comments.title: Misleading comparison of RFA-T1 and RFA-T8 robustness
comments.quote: Notice that RFA-T1 is more robust to the mimic attack than RFA-T8 in Figure 3 because more inner iterations lead to better approximate geometric median and less robust to normalized mean attacks.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that more accurate geometric median estimation (RFA-T8) reduces robustness to mimic attacks, implying that approximation error is beneficial. This is misleading: the core vulnerability stems not from estimation accuracy but from the geometric median’s inherent sensitivity to clustered non-iid data under mimic attacks. A better approximation does not cause failure—the failure is due to the attack exploiting data heterogeneity. The phrasing risks attributing robustness to algorithmic imprecision, which could misguide future design choices. The observation may be empirically valid, but the causal explanation is flawed and oversimplified.

comments.title: Incorrect robustness condition for resampling-augmented KRUM in Theorem II

comments.explanation: The condition $ 2sf + 3 \leq n $ is misleading. Since each of the $ T = n $ resampled gradients can be corrupted independently and up to $ sf $ total corruptions are possible (from $ f $ Byzantine workers each contributing up to $ s $ times), KRUM must tolerate $ sf $ corrupt inputs. Standard KRUM requires $ T \geq 2(sf) + 3 $, and since $ T = n $, the correct condition should be $ n \geq 2sf + 3 $. The paper’s phrasing $ 2sf + 3 \leq n $ is mathematically equivalent but obscures the reasoning by not expressing it in terms of $ T $. This could mislead readers into thinking the resilience scales with $ s $ without considering the role of $ T $, which is critical for understanding the method’s robustness guarantees.

comments.title: Lack of justification for almost sure convergence under dependent resampling

comments.explanation: The paper claims almost sure convergence by analogy to Blanchard et al. (2017), but the resampled gradients are not independent due to the bounded reuse of worker gradients (each used at most $ s $ times). Standard convergence proofs for robust SGD rely on i.i.d. or independent noise assumptions for martingale analysis. The dependence induced by Algorithm 2 violates this assumption, and while the paper acknowledges non-independence, it provides no justification for how the convergence analysis adapts to this. Without addressing the dependence structure in the proof, the claim remains unsubstantiated in the main text and raises concerns about the validity of the theoretical result.

comments.explanation: The text conflates gradient-based and model-based updates. RSA and signum are gradient aggregation methods that operate on normalized or signed gradients. The passage incorrectly suggests that these methods transmit local *models* (**x**_i) rather than gradients, and even explicitly adds '(not gradient)', contradicting the paper's own setup where workers send gradients. No known variants of RSA or signum in the cited works use model transmission in this context. This indicates a fundamental misunderstanding or a severe typo that misrepresents prior work and creates confusion about the communication protocol.

comments.explanation: The paper cites Xie et al. The attack in Section 3.2, if it involves sending normalized differences like $ \alpha \cdot (\bar{\mathbf{g}} - \mathbf{g}_i) $, operates on a different principle. While both may exploit non-iidness, the mechanisms are distinct. The citation may refer to omniscient adversarial knowledge rather than the attack form. comments.quote: Suppose that worker 0 is Byzantine and the remaining workers {_ 1 _, . . . ,_ 2 _n}_ are good, with a total of 2 _n_ + 1 workers. While possible in theory, this makes the failure mode seem more catastrophic than typical. More critically, in higher dimensions, projecting onto a single direction and applying coordinate-wise median (as in CM) could still give a reasonable point despite heterogeneity. The example is correct as written—technically, the median can return 1—but it conflates a carefully engineered worst case with inherent algorithmic failure in realistic non-iid settings. The result is intuitively appealing but insufficiently justified for a theoretical claim.

comments.title: Mismatch between algorithm description and theoretical assumption in Definition A
comments.quote: Let G = G(ξ) be an independent random variable which randomly selects a good worker i and samples a vector from Di and E G = **g** .
comments.explanation: In Definition A, the estimator G is defined as selecting a random good worker and sampling a gradient from its local data, with expectation **g**. However, this conflicts with the setup in Section 1 where the true objective is the average of individual losses, and thus the target gradient should be the average of the µ_i across good workers: $\mu = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in \text{good}} \mu_i$. The definition implies G samples from a random worker, so $\mathbb{E}[G] = \frac{1}{n_g} \sum_{i \in \text{good}} \mu_i$, which matches the population mean only if all good workers have equal weight. But in unbalanced data regimes or non-uniform sampling, this would not hold. Furthermore, in the resampling scheme, gradients are aggregated from a selected set, not drawn per update from a random worker. The definition thus models a different estimator than what is actually used in Algorithm 1. This weakens the theoretical grounding: while (α, f)-Byzantine resilience is an important condition, proving it for KRUM after resampling requires connecting the resampled average to the population mean, not just the mean of a randomly sampled worker. The paper appears to conflate two different population targets.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about normalized-mean aggregation in RSA and signum
comments.quote: Other methods such as RSA (Li et al., 2019) or signum (Bernstein et al., 2018) normalize entries coordinate-wise before taking a majority vote i.e. update the server model **x**_0 on server using local model **x**_i from node _i_ (not gradient) using
comments.explanation: At first glance, this passage appears to conflate two distinct concepts: gradient normalization and model update via sign of gradients. The sentence starts by discussing coordinate-wise normalization used in RSA and signum, which are gradient-based methods. However, it then shifts to describing an update using local *models* **x**_i rather than gradients, which contradicts the established setting where workers send gradients, not models. This creates confusion about whether the update rule refers to model averaging or gradient aggregation. While sign-based methods like signSGD do use the sign of the gradient (not the model), the phrase 'using local model **x**_i from node _i_ (not gradient)' suggests a different mechanism altogether. Given that the paper's setup explicitly defines worker outputs as gradients (e.g., **g**_i = ∇L_i(**x**, ξ_i^j)), this reference to model transmission is inconsistent. Even if this is a typo and 'model' should be 'gradient', the subsequent phrase '(not gradient)' directly negates that interpretation. No known version of RSA or signum in the cited works replaces gradient communication with model communication in this context. Thus, the claim misrepresents how these algorithms operate, suggesting a methodological confusion.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of mimic attack in heterogeneous setting
comments.quote: This motivates our _mimic_ attack in which all Byzantine workers collude and agree to always send gradients from the same worker. We define a specialized attack, called _mimic2_ , where half of the good workers have same datasets and send _**g**_ 1 while the rest good workers send _**g**_ 2; then all Byzantine workers send _**v**_ = _**g**_ 1 such that the geometric median of the gradients received by the server is always _**g**_ 1.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly observe that median-based methods fail when good workers’ gradients are bimodal due to heterogeneity. The mimic attack idea—Byzantine workers imitating one of the modes—is intuitive. However, the description is ambiguous in a way that could mislead. The phrase 'send gradients from the same worker' is vague: do they copy a specific worker’s gradient vector, or sample from one worker’s data distribution? Given the setting, the latter is more realistic, but the paper doesn't clarify. More importantly, the example assumes a perfect bimodal split (*half* the good workers send **g**_1, the other half **g**_2) and that Byzantines deterministically send **g**_1. In high-dimensional space, exact equality of gradient vectors (**g**_i = **g**_j) almost never occurs; gradients are random vectors. The geometric median is continuous—if **g**_1 and **g**_2 are well-separated, inserting f copies of a vector near **g**_1 will shift the median toward **g**_1 but not make it 'always **g**_1'. The claim 'geometric median ... is always **g**_1' overstates the attack’s determinism and ignores continuity. A careful reader might think the median becomes exactly **g**_1, which is only possible in degenerate cases. The correct interpretation is that the median is biased toward the larger cluster, but the phrasing suggests an absolute outcome. This is a minor overclaim given the illustrative intent, but could mislead in theoretical analysis.

comments.title: Misleading connection between Xie et al. (2019b) and normalized mean attack
comments.quote: Further our normalized mean attack Section 3.2 is inspired by (Xie et al., 2019b).
comments.explanation: The paper positions its 'normalized mean attack' as novel and inspired by Xie et al. (2019b). However, Xie et al. (2019b) — 'Generalized Byzantine-tolerant SGD' — introduces the 'Byzantine Gradient Descent' (BGD) attack, which is a *directional* attack: Byzantine workers send a fixed vector aligned with a malicious direction, not one derived from normalization. The attack described in Section 3.2 appears to be a 'normalized sign flip' or coordinate-shift attack, possibly akin to what's shown in Figure 1b. But the text doesn't specify the exact form of the attack beyond referencing an equation not shown. If the attack involves sending normalized gradients of the form α · (**g**̄ - **g**_i) or similar (as in later figures), the conceptual link to Xie et al. (2019b) is weak: that paper uses *additive* adversarial directions, not *normalized differences*. While both exploit non-iidness, the mechanism differs. More critically, a 'normalized mean attack' is not a standard term, and no such attack is described in Xie et al. (2019b). The citation may be referring to the use of omniscient knowledge (full system state) rather than the attack vector itself. While the authors are justified in drawing conceptual inspiration, stating the attack is 'inspired by' without clarifying the nature could mislead readers into thinking it's a direct adaptation. Given that novel attacks are a key contribution, this weak citation risks overstating the lineage.

comments.title: Inconsistent resampling output notation in Algorithm 2
comments.explanation: There is a clear notation mismatch in Algorithm 2: the code computes and refers to **g**_t as the average, but the return statement outputs { **g**_¯_t : t ∈ [T] }, using a barred symbol not seen in the computation. Given the context (Proposition I uses **g**_¯_t), the bar is intentional and denotes the resampled average. However, inside the algorithm, the variable is computed as **g**_t without a bar, creating a contradiction. This is likely an OCR or typesetting artifact where the bar was lost in one instance. But even so, the internal variable should be **g**_¯_t to match the returned value and align with Proposition I, which depends on this notation. The mismatch could confuse a reader attempting to implement the algorithm—should the result be called **g**_t or **g**_¯_t? While the surrounding context implies they are the same, the algorithm as written uses inconsistent naming. A reproducing reader might not know whether this indicates a different variable or a typo. Given that notation consistency is critical in distributed algorithms, this is a real issue.

comments.title: Incorrect expression for variance in Proposition I
comments.quote: var(\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t) = \frac{n-1}{sn-1} \sigma^2 \quad \forall t \in [T] \quad (8)
comments.explanation: Initially, I tried to verify the claim in Proposition I regarding the variance of the resampled gradient averages. The authors state that after resampling $ s $ gradients without replacement from $ n $ workers, the variance of each averaged gradient $ \bar{\mathbf{g}}_t $ is $ \frac{n-1}{sn-1} \sigma^2 $. However, this expression appears incorrect. From sampling theory, when drawing a sample of size $ s $ without replacement from a population of size $ n $, the variance of the sample mean is $ \frac{\sigma^2}{s} \cdot \frac{n-s}{n-1} $, which includes a finite population correction factor. Here, the authors seem to be treating the resampled gradients as estimates of a population of size $ sn $, but since gradients are reused at most $ s $ times and the total number of unique gradients is still $ n $, this interpretation is misleading. The actual variance depends on the second-order structure of the selection process in Algorithm 2. In particular, because sampling stops when any gradient has been selected $ s $ times (line 'if c[ji] == s: break'), the resampling is not a simple uniform draw over all subsets, and the independence assumptions underlying standard sampling formulas break down. This complicates the derivation of the exact variance. Furthermore, Remark 1 states that increasing $ s $ reduces variance by approximately $ 1/s $, which aligns with $ \text{var}(\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t) \propto 1/s $, but the given expression $ \frac{n-1}{sn-1} \sigma^2 $ behaves like $ \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{n-1}{n - 1/s} \approx \frac{1}{s} $ only asymptotically, and even then, the formula doesn't match standard finite-population sampling results. This suggests either an OCR error or an incorrect derivation. Crucially, the subsequent analysis (e.g., Theorem II) likely depends on the correct scaling of this variance, so the error undermines the theoretical support for the resampling scheme.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 description conflicts with claimed sampling scheme
comments.quote: Compute average $\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t := \frac{1}{s} \sum_{i=1}^s \mathbf{g}_{j_i}$
comments.explanation: The paper claims that Algorithm 2 implements 's-resampling without replacement', where each gradient is sampled at most $ s $ times. However, the pseudocode contains a while loop with a break condition that depends on whether a particular index has been selected $ s $ times. The inner loop runs for $ i := 1,\dots,s $ and selects indices $ j_i \sim \text{Uniform}([n]) $ with a check on counter $ c[j_i] $. The condition 'if c[ji] == s: break' exits the inner loop prematurely if any index reaches the selection limit. This means that some $ \bar{\mathbf{g}}_t $ may be computed from fewer than $ s $ gradients — but the algorithm still divides by $ s $, leading to a biased estimate. Moreover, the outer loop iterates $ T = n $ times (as set in the input), but there's no guarantee that $ T $ averaged gradients can be formed under the selection constraint, especially when $ s=1 $. For example, when $ s=1 $, only $ n $ total selections are allowed, so only $ T=n/s=n $ means can be formed, but the selection process could stall if all gradients are selected before $ T $ averages are computed. This results in an underspecified and potentially broken algorithm. The description in Section 4 says resampling produces $ T $ averaged gradients, but the pseudocode does not ensure this. The algorithm should either use proper reservoir sampling or resample with replacement unless a full buffer is maintained. The current version could fail to produce $ T $ outputs or generate biased averages.

comments.title: Mimic attack construction lacks formal connection to geometric median
comments.quote: all Byzantine workers send $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{g}_1$ such that the geometric median of the gradients received by the server is always $\mathbf{g}_1$.
comments.explanation: The authors describe the 'mimic2' attack, where Byzantine workers send $ \mathbf{g}_1 $ to force the geometric median to output $ \mathbf{g}_1 $. However, the geometric median is not simply the mode or majority vote; it is a continuous estimator in $ \mathbb{R}^d $. For arbitrary $ \mathbf{g}_1 $ and $ \mathbf{g}_2 $, sending $ \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{g}_1 $ does not guarantee the geometric median equals $ \mathbf{g}_1 $. In fact, the geometric median minimizes total distance, so its value depends on the positions of all points. For instance, if good workers send $ \mathbf{g}_1 $ and $ \mathbf{g}_2 $ in balanced numbers, and adversaries also send $ \mathbf{g}_1 $, the median could lie somewhere on the line segment between $ \mathbf{g}_1 $ and $ \mathbf{g}_2 $, not necessarily $ \mathbf{g}_1 $. The attack would only succeed if the adversary mass tips the balance significantly — but since $ f $ is small, this may not happen. The claim that 'the geometric median is always $ \mathbf{g}_1 $' is therefore unjustified without stronger assumptions on gradient magnitudes or directions. While the idea of mimicking is plausible, this specific construction overreaches. The left plot of Figure 1c is cited as evidence, but without seeing the actual figure, the claim rests solely on this flawed reasoning.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Definition A regarding estimator G
comments.quote: Let G = G(\boldsymbol{\xi}) be an independent random variable which randomly selects a good worker i and samples a vector from Di and E G = \mathbf{g}.
comments.explanation: In Definition A, the authors introduce $ G $ as an estimator of good gradients, defined by randomly selecting a good worker $ i $ and sampling a vector from $ D_i $, with $ \mathbb{E} G = \mathbf{g} $. However, $ \mathbf{g} $ is not defined here. From earlier notation, $ \boldsymbol{\mu}_i $ denotes the mean of $ \mathbf{g}_i $, and $ \mathbf{g} $ might be intended to denote the average of $ \boldsymbol{\mu}_i $'s. But the condition $ \mathbb{E} G = \mathbf{g} $ is only meaningful if $ \mathbf{g} $ is properly defined. Moreover, the rest of the definition uses $ \{V_i \in D_i\} $ as the good gradients, making the role of $ G $ unclear — is it a reference gradient or a hypothetical sample? The connection between $ G $ and the aggregation rule's input is underspecified, weakening the foundation of the subsequent resilience definition. While context suggests $ \mathbf{g} $ should be $ \nabla L(\mathbf{x}) $, the omission harms clarity, especially for readers trying to verify the alignment with Blanchard et al. (2017).

comments.title: Inconsistent use of $ F_i $ in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Each good worker $i$ randomly samples a datapoint $j$ and computes a stochastic gradient $\mathbf{g}_i := \nabla F_i(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\xi}_i^j)$
comments.explanation: In the paper's preamble, the loss function for worker $ i $ is defined as $ L_i(\mathbf{x}) := \frac{1}{m_i} \sum_{j=1}^{m_i} L_i(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\xi}_i^j) $, so $ L_i $ is used consistently as the individual loss. However, in Algorithm 1, the gradient computation uses $ F_i $ instead of $ L_i $. This is a clear notation inconsistency. Given the context, $ F_i $ is almost certainly a typo and should be $ L_i $, since no $ F_i $ is defined in the paper. This could mislead a careful reader into thinking $ F_i $ represents a different function (e.g., local objective or regularized loss), especially since Section 2 mentions RSA using an $ \ell_1 $ penalty — but even then, $ F_i $ is not defined. The use of $ F_i $ here contradicts earlier notation and is not justified. While likely an OCR error or author oversight, it violates the principle of consistent notation.

comments.title: Incorrect Expression for Resampling Variance in Theorem II
comments.quote: σ²(x₀) := ≤ α\frac{n−s}{n−1} \frac{1}{s} \tilde{σ} π/2[(2x such)]
comments.explanation: The expression for the variance after resampling in the statement of Theorem II is garbled, likely due to OCR error or formatting issues. In the context, Equation (8) correctly states that var(ḡₜ) = \frac{n−s}{n−1} \cdot \frac{1}{s} σ². The intent appears to be referencing this reduced variance in the convergence analysis, as Remark 1 highlights this factor as critical. However, the passage presents a malformed inequality involving α, s, n, and a mysterious \tilde{σ}, alongside a dangling 'π/2' and unbalanced brackets. While the overall convergence claim likely depends on the correct variance reduction factor, this version is mathematically incoherent. Even assuming OCR errors (e.g., '\frac{n−s}{n−1} \cdot \frac{1}{s} \sigma^2'), the inequality structure and surrounding text ('there exists awhereconstant') are nonsensical. This appears to be a corrupted sentence that fails to assert a proper condition on the noise level for convergence, undermining the technical justification for the almost sure convergence claim.

comments.title: Unresolved Dependency in Theorem II’s Condition (iv)
comments.quote: (iv) there exists awhereconstant σ²(x₀) := ≤ α\frac{n−s}{n−1} \frac{1}{s} \tilde{σ} π/2[(2x such)]
comments.explanation: Condition (iv) in Theorem II attempts to bound the variance of the resampled gradients but fails syntactically and semantically. The phrase 'there exists awhereconstant' is clearly corrupted. The intended meaning, given Proposition I and Remark 1, is likely that the variance of the resampled gradients is bounded by \frac{n−s}{n−1} \cdot \frac{1}{s} σ², and this bound is used in the convergence analysis. However, the use of \tilde{σ} instead of σ is inconsistent with earlier notation (no \tilde{σ} defined), and the presence of α — an angular parameter from the (α,f)-resilience definition — in a variance expression suggests a deeper confusion or corruption. In formal convergence proofs, such as in Blanchard et al. (2017), bounded gradient variance is a standard requirement. Here, the condition conflates the variance bound with the angular tolerance α and ends incoherently. Without a clean statement bounding the second moment of the resampled gradients, the proof’s reliance on moment conditions (iii) cannot be properly extended to the resampled setting.

comments.title: Undefined and Incoherent Final Condition in Theorem II
comments.quote: (v) finally, beyond a certain horizon,] that for all x we have η(T, sf) ·[ ∥] √[x][∥] d₂ · σ[≥] (D x ) [, there exist] ≤∥∇L( x ε > ) ∥·[ 0] sin [ and] α₀ ≤ β < π/2 − α such that ∥∇L(x)∥≥ ε > 0 and ⟨x, ∇L(x)⟩≥ cos β∥x∥· ∥∇L(x)∥
comments.explanation: The final assumption (v) in Theorem II is severely malformed, making it impossible to interpret. It attempts to state a growth or coercivity condition on the loss function — common in stochastic convergence analyses — ensuring that the gradient doesn’t vanish outside a compact set. The intended claim, based on Blanchard et al. (2017), is likely that ⟨x, ∇L(x)⟩ ≥ cos β ∥x∥ ∥∇L(x)∥ and ∥∇L(x)∥ ≥ ε for large ∥x∥, which prevents the iterates from diverging. However, the current text is fragmented by OCR artifacts (e.g., '·[ ∥] √', 'σ[≥] (D x )', '[ 0] sin'), contains syntactic garbage ('there exist] ≤∥∇L'), and abruptly ends. The function η(T, sf) is also undefined — it may refer to a robustness parameter dependent on the number of resampled gradients and corrupted ones, but this is not specified. While the core idea of coercivity may be present in intent, the expression is not usable as a mathematical condition, and a reader cannot verify whether the proof in Appendix A depends on a correct version.

comments.title: Incorrect Condition on Number of Byzantine Workers in Theorem II
comments.quote: If s > 1 and 2sf + 3 ≤ n, then
comments.explanation: The condition 2sf + 3 ≤ n appears too strong and likely misstates the robustness of KRUM. Standard KRUM requires n ≥ 2f + 3 to tolerate f Byzantine workers, as each worker must be close to at least n−f−2 others. When composed with resampling, up to sf of the T resampled gradients may be corrupted (as noted in the summary). Therefore, for KRUM ◦ Resampling to work, we need T ≥ 2(sf) + 3, assuming KRUM is applied to T inputs with up to sf Byzantines. The paper sets T = n in Algorithm 2, so the condition should be n ≥ 2sf + 3, not 2sf + 3 ≤ n (which is always true if the former holds). However, stating it as '2sf + 3 ≤ n' is syntactically correct but misleading: it omits the necessary context that T = n and implies the condition is on the original worker count rather than the resampled gradient count. The authors’ intent is likely correct (requiring enough resampled gradients to tolerate sf corrupted ones), but the way it’s written conflates the resampling parameter s with the original Byzantine count f without clarifying the role of T. This could mislead a reader into thinking the resilience scales multiplicatively in s without increasing T, which is not the case.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of G in (α,f)-Byzantine Resilience
comments.quote: Let G = G(ξ) be an independent random variable which randomly selects a good worker i and samples a vector from Di
comments.explanation: In Definition A, G is described as a random selector of a good worker's gradient, but this is ambiguous in the resampling context. In the original Blanchard et al. (2017), resilience is defined with respect to the true gradient ∇L(x). Here, the resampled gradients are averages of s worker gradients, so the population mean becomes μ = (1/n)∑μ_i, and each resampled gradient ḡ_t has E[ḡ_t] = μ. The definition should clarify whether G represents a single worker’s gradient or the resampled gradient distribution. The text says ‘estimator of the good gradients’ — plural — suggesting it may refer to the resampled averages. However, the sampling procedure described (selecting one worker) does not match the resampled input to AGGR. This mismatch could lead a reader to believe the resilience is defined at the worker level, not the resampled gradient level. While the context suggests AGGR ◦ Resampling operates on the set {ḡ_t}, the formal definition does not reflect this, risking a gap between the assumed model and the theoretical framework.

comments.title: Missing Justification for Almost Sure Convergence Claim
comments.quote: Then we can conclude the almost sure convergence similar to (Blanchard et al., 2017, Proposition 2)
comments.explanation: The authors state they can conclude almost sure convergence ‘similar to’ Blanchard et al. (2017), but the resampled gradients {ḡ_t} are not independent — only identically distributed — due to sampling without replacement and bounded reuse (s times per gradient). Standard convergence proofs for SGD with robust aggregation, including Blanchard et al., assume i.i.d. gradient estimates or bounded i.i.d. noise. The dependence introduced by resampling violates this assumption. The paper acknowledges the lack of independence in Section 4 (“not independent”) but does not address how this affects the martingale-based convergence analysis. While the variance reduction may help, the dependence structure could invalidate the Lyapunov function analysis used in such proofs. The deferment to Appendix A is noted, but without seeing it, the claim appears undersupported in the main text. Given that dependence can significantly affect convergence in stochastic optimization, this is a notable gap in justification.

comments.title: Inconsistency in geometric median output under mimic2 attack
comments.quote: Then all Byzantine workers send $ \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{g}_1 $ such that the geometric median of the gradients received by the server is always $ \mathbf{g}_1 $.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the claim that the geometric median outputs exactly $ \mathbf{g}_1 $ under the Mimic2 attack seems strong. The authors' intent is clear: they want to show that symmetry in good worker gradients ($ \mathbf{g}_1 $ and $ \mathbf{g}_2 $) can be exploited by Byzantine workers sending $ \mathbf{g}_1 $, skewing the median. However, the geometric median is not generally guaranteed to output one of the input points unless under specific symmetry or low-dimensional conditions. In high dimensions, the geometric median is typically a point in the convex hull but not necessarily equal to $ \mathbf{g}_1 $. While the attack may still bias the median toward $ \mathbf{g}_1 $, asserting it outputs $ \mathbf{g}_1 $ exactly is an overstatement unless additional assumptions (e.g., one-dimensional gradients or specific configurations) are made. The surrounding context defines mimic2 precisely as causing the median to output $ \mathbf{g}_1 $, but this amounts to assuming the conclusion rather than deriving it. This weakens the theoretical strength of the attack model, even if empirically valid.

comments.title: Unjustified variance reduction formula in resampling
comments.quote: var(\bar{\mathbf{g}}_t) = \frac{n-s}{n-1} \cdot \frac{1}{s} \sigma^2
comments.explanation: The formula for variance after resampling appears in the summary as Equation 扫黑除 (which is clearly an OCR error for a numbered equation, likely (8)). The expression $ \frac{n-s}{n-1} \cdot \frac{1}{s} \sigma^2 $ resembles finite population correction in sampling without replacement. In standard sampling theory, the variance of the sample mean of $ s $ draws without replacement from a finite population of size $ n $ with variance $ \sigma^2 $ is $ \frac{\sigma^2}{s} \cdot \frac{n-s}{n-1} $. This matches the formula, suggesting correctness. However, in Algorithm 2, the resampling is done $ T = n $ times with a global counter $ c[i] $ limiting each gradient to $ s $ uses. This means the samples are *dependent* across $ t $, and not independent draws. The stated variance assumes each $ \bar{\mathbf{g}}_t $ is formed from an independent resample, which contradicts the algorithm's bounded replacement design where gradients are consumed. The derivation in Proposition I treats the process as if resampling is with finite replacement but independent across $ t $, which is inconsistent with Algorithm 2’s sequential sampling with a shared counter. This dependency invalidates the independence assumption needed for the stated variance formula. The authors correctly identify the mean, but the variance derivation ignores the sampling-without-replacement dependency structure. This is a non-trivial statistical error that affects the theoretical justification of variance reduction.

comments.title: Misleading comparison between RFA-T1 and RFA-T8 robustness
comments.quote: Notice that RFA-T1 is more robust to the mimic attack than RFA-T8 in Figure 3 because more inner iterations lead to better approximate geometric median and less robust to normalized mean attacks.
comments.explanation: The passage claims that RFA-T8 (with more inner iterations) is *less robust* to the mimic attack because it computes a better geometric median, which sounds counterintuitive—shouldn't better estimation improve robustness? The authors may mean that a more accurate geometric median is *more vulnerable* to the mimic2 attack when the Byzantine gradient aligns with a cluster (here, $ \mathbf{g}_1 $), because the median is no longer pulled toward the true population center. This is plausible, but the phrasing 'better approximate geometric median' suggests algorithmic improvement, while the context implies it's a *liability* under attack. The claim that a better approximation leads to worse robustness could mislead readers into thinking approximation error is protective, when in fact the core issue is the attack's exploitation of data heterogeneity. The explanation confuses cause: it’s not the *approximation quality* that reduces robustness, but the *inherent bias* of the geometric median under non-iid data combined with the mimic strategy. The statement oversimplifies and risks misattribution of failure mode.

comments.title: Undefined parameter $ \eta(T, sf) $ in assumption
comments.quote: and $ \eta(T, sf) \cdot \|\mathbf{x}\| \cdot d^{-2} \cdot \sigma \geq \varepsilon_0 $
comments.explanation: In the list of assumptions, the term $ \eta(T, sf) $ appears in a critical growth condition but is never defined in the provided context. While $ T $ and $ sf $ are known (number of resamples and maximum corrupted gradients), $ \eta $ is unexplained. It may be a scaling factor or a function related to convergence, but its form is essential for verifying the assumption's plausibility. The surrounding assumption is used in Theorem II for convergence, so its underspecification affects reproducibility. The paper may define $ \eta $ in a missing section (e.g., Appendix), but within the provided text, it’s a gap. This is particularly problematic because the inequality involves multiple variables and appears in a key technical condition. A reader cannot assess whether this holds in practice or how it scales.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a timely and critical gap in the literature by investigating Byzantine robustness in the more realistic setting of heterogeneous (non-IID) data distributions, a scenario where existing defenses are known to struggle. The motivation is strong and clearly articulated, and the proposal of novel attacks that exploit this heterogeneity, alongside a resampling-based defense, represents a valuable contribution to the field of robust federated learning. However, the manuscript suffers from distracting formatting errors and typos (e.g., LaTeX artifacts and misspellings like "vecter") that undermine its clarity and polish. Furthermore, while the authors claim the resampling scheme can be combined with "any existing robust aggregation rule," the introduction only explicitly mentions a theoretical instantiation with KRUM; without seeing the full theoretical analysis, it is unclear if the proposed solution offers the same theoretical guarantees for median or sign-based defenses, or if it relies primarily on empirical heuristics. A stronger theoretical justification for the general applicability of the resampling method would be necessary to elevate this work beyond a practical engineering trick.

comments.title: OCR errors in optimization problem formulation
comments.explanation: The mathematical notation for the optimization problem contains significant OCR errors that obscure the intended meaning. Based on context and standard notation in distributed optimization, this should likely read: L* = min_{x ∈ R^d} L(x) := (1/n) Σ_{i=1}^n L_i(x). The garbled notation makes it difficult to understand the fundamental problem formulation, which is critical for understanding the paper's contributions.

comments.title: OCR errors in individual loss function definition
comments.explanation: The definition of the individual loss function contains OCR errors. Based on standard empirical risk minimization notation and context, this should likely be: L_i(x) := (1/m_i) Σ_{j=1}^{m_i} L_i(x, ξ_i^j). The OCR artifacts make this fundamental definition unclear.

comments.title: OCR errors in variance formula in Proposition I
comments.quote: var(¯g t) = snn−− 11 [σ][2]
comments.explanation: The variance formula in Proposition I contains OCR errors that make it unreadable. Based on sampling theory and the context about reducing heterogeneity, this should likely express how resampling reduces variance by a factor related to s. The garbled formula undermines the theoretical contribution about variance reduction.

comments.title: OCR errors in Algorithm 2 pseudocode
comments.quote: while Select ji ∼ Uniform ([ n ]) do if c [ ji ] < s then c [ ji ] += 1 If c [ ji ] ==¯ s Break; s Compute average ¯ g t :=[1] s  i =1 [g][j] i Return { ¯ g t : t ∈ [ T ] } , {ji[t][:] [ t][ ∈][[] [T][]] [,][ i][ ∈][[] [s][]] [}]
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 contains significant OCR errors that make the resampling procedure unclear. Key parts like the loop structure, termination conditions, and return values are garbled. This is problematic as Algorithm 2 is central to the paper's main contribution.

comments.title: Missing factor in variance formula of Proposition I
comments.quote: $var(\bar{g}_t) = \frac{n-s}{n-1} \sigma^2$
comments.explanation: The formula for the variance of the resampled gradient $\bar{g}_t$ in Proposition I appears to be missing a factor of $1/s$. The variance of the sample mean for a sample of size $s$ drawn without replacement from a finite population of size $n$ with variance $\sigma^2$ is given by $Var(\bar{g}_t) = \frac{\sigma^2}{s} \frac{n-s}{n-1}$. The formula in the text lists only the finite population correction factor $\frac{n-s}{n-1} \sigma^2$. This omission contradicts the explanation in Remark 1 immediately following the proposition, which states that 'increasing s leads to the resulting resampled gradients being a better estimator... [and] reduces the heterogeneity (variance) by approximately 1/s'. If the formula in the proposition were correct as written (without $1/s$), the variance would not scale with $1/s$.

comments.title: Discrepancy between Algorithm 2 pseudocode and Proposition 1 variance derivation
comments.quote: Algorithm 2 Resampling with s-replacement ... Select ji ∼ Uniform([n]) ... Proposition I ... var(¯g_t) = snn−− 11 σ^2 ...
comments.explanation: Proposition I derives the variance of the resampled gradients as $\text{var}(\bar{g}_t) = \frac{n-1}{sn-1}\sigma^2$. This formula corresponds to sampling $s$ items *without replacement* from a population of size $N=sn$ (where each of the $n$ original gradients appears $s$ times). However, the pseudocode in Algorithm 2 instructs to `Select ji ∼ Uniform([n])`. Sampling indices uniformly from $[n]$ implies sampling the original gradients *with replacement* (effectively, subject to the global cap of $s$), which yields a different variance (specifically, the samples are independent until caps are hit, leading to a variance of approximately $\sigma^2/s$, which is higher than the claimed $\frac{n-1}{sn-1}\sigma^2$ for $s>1$). The text description 's-resampling without replacement' supports the Proposition I derivation, but the pseudocode `Uniform([n])` contradicts this. A correct implementation for the derived formula would require sampling uniformly from the multiset of $sn$ items, not uniformly from the $n$ indices.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical and timely gap in the field of distributed optimization: the failure of Byzantium-robust defense mechanisms under the realistic, non-i.i.d. data distributions found in federated learning environments. The motivation is clear, and the proposed resampling scheme offers a pragmatically elegant "plug-and-play" solution that conceptually bridges the gap between heterogeneous data and existing robust aggregation rules. The introduction is well-structured, clearly outlining the limitation of current state-of-the-art defenses and establishing the theoretical relevance of the proposed approach. However, a significant issue the authors must address in the subsequent sections is the formal definition of the "resampling" mechanism itself—specifically, how this process is implemented without violating privacy constraints or introducing significant communication overhead in a practical federated setting. Furthermore, while the study claims that existing rules fail even in the absence of adversaries under heterogeneity, the paper must carefully distinguish between performance regressions caused simply by non-i.i.d. noise versus those caused specifically by the adversarial exploitation of that non-i.i.d. variance to be considered truly robust. If the technical proof of convergence for the resampling scheme is as tractable and rigorous as the authors suggest, this work represents a substantial contribution to the field.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a timely and practically critical gap in Byzantine-robust distributed learning by highlighting the catastrophic failure of existing defenses under realistic non-IID data distributions and proposing a simple resampling-based mitigation strategy. The motivation is clearly articulated and well-positioned within the current literature, and the introduction of a computationally lightweight adaptation layer is a compelling high-level contribution. However, several significant issues must be rigorously resolved to meet the standard for publication. First, it remains unclear whether the proposed resampling scheme fundamentally corrects the statistical bias inherent to non-IID federated optimization or merely acts as a heuristic variance-reduction mechanism that could inadvertently amplify local drift or introduce new sampling biases depending on implementation parameters. Second, the theoretical analysis currently appears limited to instantiating KRUM, leaving the generalization to coordinate-wise median and geometric-median aggregators—which constitute the majority of recent robust methods—unsubstantiated. While the writing is generally clear and the mathematical setup is standard, the authors must provide a more rigorous analysis of how resampling interacts with both non-IID gradient statistics and adversarial signal structures, clarify its convergence and computational trade-offs, and validate its effectiveness across a broader spectrum of robust aggregation rules and attack models.

comments.title: Missing explicit constraint on the number of resampled gradients $T$ in Theorem II
comments.quote: If s >_ 1 _and_ 2 _sf_ + 3 _\u2264 n, then_
comments.explanation: The theorem states a validity condition on $n$, but KRUM operates on the output of the resampling step, which produces exactly $T$ gradients. For KRUM to satisfy its robustness guarantees, the number of inputs must satisfy $T \ge 2f' + 3$, where $f' \le sf$ is the maximum number of corrupted resampled gradients (per Proposition I). While Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2 implicitly set $T=n$ in their setup, the theorem statement itself omits this requirement. A reader might incorrectly apply the theorem with $T < n$ or $T < 2sf+3$, leading to invalid convergence guarantees or false confidence in small-$T$ regimes. I suggest explicitly adding $T \ge 2sf + 3$ to the theorem's premise.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing obscures the robustness trade-off between RFA-T1 and RFA-T8
comments.quote: Notice that RFA-T1 is more robust to the mimic attack than RFA-T8 in Figure 3 because more inner iterations lead to better approximate geometric median and less robust to normalized mean attacks.
comments.explanation: The sentence is syntactically ambiguous and conflates two distinct vulnerability mechanisms. As written, it grammatically implies that 'more inner iterations lead to ... less robust to normalized mean attacks,' which contradicts standard robust aggregation intuition: approximating a geometric median (higher T) typically *improves* robustness against mean-based perturbations, whereas approximating an arithmetic mean (lower T, i.e., RFA-T1) makes the method *less* robust to the normalized mean attack described in Section 3.2. The authors likely intend to highlight a trade-off: RFA-T8's convergence to a geometric median makes it more vulnerable to the mimic attack (Section 3.3), whereas RFA-T1's behavior resembles arithmetic mean aggregation, making it more vulnerable to the normalized mean attack. Explicitly separating these points (e.g., using 'whereas' or explicitly naming the subject of 'less robust') would clarify the causal relationships and prevent readers from misinterpreting the robustness profile of higher-T iterations.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: Based on the provided excerpt, the paper addresses an important and timely problem—Byzantine robustness under heterogeneous (non-iid) data—and makes a clear case for the failure of existing methods in this setting. The high-level writing is generally clear and well-structured, with a logical flow from motivation to contributions and related work. However, several significant issues are apparent. First, the claim that most current defenses assume identical data distributions is somewhat overstated, as the related work itself cites recent efforts (e.g., Data & Diggavi, 2020) that explicitly relax this assumption; the paper would benefit from a more nuanced positioning of its novelty relative to these works. Second, while the proposed resampling scheme is described as “simple,” the excerpt does not provide enough detail to assess its theoretical grounding or practical computational cost—the key novelty may be too incremental if resampling merely pre-processes gradients without addressing the core algorithmic challenge of robustness under heterogeneity. Third, the paper introduces two new attacks (normalized gradient and mimic) but does not yet explain how they differ from existing attacks like those of Xie et al. (2019b) beyond a brief note; a clearer delineation of attack novelty and effectiveness is needed. Finally, the theoretical analysis is only mentioned for KRUM, leaving open questions about the generalizability of the proof to other aggregation rules, and the experimental validation is not previewed in sufficient detail to gauge rigor. Overall, the paper has a reasonable foundation but requires stronger differentiation from prior work, deeper theoretical justification, and a more thorough exposition of the proposed method to meet the high standards of a top conference.

comments.title: Misleading citation of RSA (Li et al., 2019) as a normalized aggregation scheme
comments.quote: ii) schemes which use normalized means (e.g. RSA (Li et al., 2019)), and iii) those which use the median (e.g. RFA (Pillutla et al., 2019)).
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the authors categorize RSA as a 'normalized means' scheme. However, in Section 3.2, they describe RSA as normalizing entries coordinate-wise before a majority vote (sign-based aggregation), not as averaging normalized vectors. This is inconsistent: RSA is a coordinate-wise sign-based method, not a 'normalized mean' (which would be something like dividing each vector by its norm then averaging). The description in 3.2 correctly captures RSA and signum, but the initial grouping in the introduction of Section 3 is misleading because it lumps RSA under 'normalized means' while later treating it as a sign-based method. This could confuse a reader about the actual mechanism of RSA.

comments.quote: However, a Byzantine worker can still craft an “omniscient” attack to foil robust aggregations, using an approach similar to the negative sum for the arithmetic mean (Blanchard et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019):
comments.explanation: The passage introduces an attack formula but the actual equation is not provided in the excerpt (it ends with a colon). The text says 'using an approach similar to the negative sum for the arithmetic mean', but does not specify what the attack vector is. The context references Figure 1b which shows results of the 'normalized mean attack', but the precise mathematical form of the attack is missing. This is an underspecified method: a reader cannot know how the Byzantine worker crafts the vector. It might be defined in a later section (e.g., Section 3.2 might continue), but as presented here, it is insufficiently described.

This is a formatting issue from OCR, but it leaves a gap in the argument.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of mimic2 attack
comments.quote: We define a specialized attack, called mimic2 , where half of the good workers have same datasets and send g_1 while the rest good workers send g_2; then all Byzantine workers send v = g_1 such that the geometric median of the gradients received by the server is always g_1.
comments.explanation: The description assumes an exact split (half and half) among good workers, which is ambiguous when the number of good workers is odd (e.g., 8 good workers as in Figure 1c). Furthermore, the phrase 'have same datasets' is ambiguous: it could mean they share identical data (unusual for non-iid) or that they coincidentally produce the same gradient g_1. The attack scenario is underspecified, and the authors do not discuss how it generalizes to more than two distinct data distributions among good workers. This undermines the claim that the attack 'breaks geometric-median-based robust aggregation rules' in realistic heterogeneous settings.

comments.title: Claim that 'only three works investigate Byzantine robustness with non-iid workers' may be too strong
comments.quote: As far as we are aware, only (Li et al., 2019; Ghosh et al., 2019; Sattler et al., 2020) explicitly investigate Byzantine robustness with non-iid workers.
comments.explanation: The authors state that only three works investigate this specific combination. However, the paper's own citation list includes other works that address non-iid settings (e.g., Xie et al., 2019a; Chen et al., 2018; Rajput et al., 2019) but these are dismissed because they 'assume that the server has the entire training dataset.' The phrasing 'explicitly investigate Byzantine robustness with non-iid workers' could be interpreted narrowly to exclude those server-controlled distribution methods. While technically accurate given the federated learning constraint, a reader might think there is a gap in the literature that is broader than it actually is. This is a minor overclaim about the novelty of the problem.

comments.title: Assumption (iii) is inconsistent with the non-iid setting
comments.quote: (iii) E j, ξ [ Gj ( x , ξ )] = ∇L ( x )
comments.explanation: The condition states that a uniformly chosen good worker's stochastic gradient has expectation equal to the gradient of the overall loss L, where L is defined as (1/n)∑L_i over all n workers (including Byzantine ones if they have data). In the heterogeneous data setting that the paper focuses on, the good workers' data distributions differ, so the average of their true gradients (over random j) does not equal the full gradient unless the Byzantine workers' contributions coincidentally match or the data distributions are identical in expectation. The paper earlier acknowledges heterogeneity (bounded variance across good workers) and designs attacks that exploit it. Assuming (iii) effectively assumes away the problem, making the theorem's applicability to the non-iid case questionable. comments.title: Inconsistent use of variance reduction factor in Proposition I
comments.quote: var(¯g_t) =  rac{n-1}{sn}σ^2
comments.explanation: The expression in Proposition I gives variance (n-1)/(s n) σ^2. In the summary, the same equation is presented as (n-1)/(s n) σ^2. However, standard sampling theory for without-replacement sampling from a finite population of size n with sample size s gives variance factor (n-s)/(n-1) * (1/s) for the sample mean. The given factor (n-1)/(s n) is non-standard. If s=2 and n=10, (n-1)/(s n) = 9/20 = 0.45, whereas the usual finite-population correction would give (n-s)/(n-1)*(1/s) = (8/9)*(1/2) ≈ 0.444. The text says σ^2 := (1/n) Σ ||g_i - μ||^2, which is population variance with divisor n. When sampling without replacement, the variance of the sample mean is ((N-n)/(N-1))*(σ²/n) for simple random sampling. For sampling s items from N=n with the given constraint (each gradient sampled at most s times), the derivation is not standard and the claim needs justification. The remark later says variance reduces by ~1/s, which holds only approximately for large n, but the exact expression may be incorrect.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: Based on the provided excerpt, the paper addresses an important and timely problem—Byzantine robustness under heterogeneous (non-iid) data—and makes a clear case for the failure of existing methods in this setting. The high-level writing is generally clear and well-structured, with a logical flow from motivation to contributions and related work. However, several significant issues are apparent. First, the claim that most current defenses assume identical data distributions is somewhat overstated, as the related work itself cites recent efforts (e.g., Data & Diggavi, 2020) that explicitly relax this assumption; the paper would benefit from a more nuanced positioning of its novelty relative to these works. Second, while the proposed resampling scheme is described as “simple,” the excerpt does not provide enough detail to assess its theoretical grounding or practical computational cost—the key novelty may be too incremental if resampling merely pre-processes gradients without addressing the core algorithmic challenge of robustness under heterogeneity. Third, the paper introduces two new attacks (normalized gradient and mimic) but does not yet explain how they differ from existing attacks like those of Xie et al. (2019b) beyond a brief note; a clearer delineation of attack novelty and effectiveness is needed. Finally, the theoretical analysis is only mentioned for KRUM, leaving open questions about the generalizability of the proof to other aggregation rules, and the experimental validation is not previewed in sufficient detail to gauge rigor. Overall, the paper has a reasonable foundation but requires stronger differentiation from prior work, deeper theoretical justification, and a more thorough exposition of the proposed method to meet the high standards of a top conference.
